confidence.disclosure: Tariq ibn Ziyad is famous for one event - the conquest of the Iberian peninsula in 711 - and the rest of his life is poorly documented. His birth and death dates are educated guesses (he is generally placed in the second half of the 7th century and the early 8th). Even his ethnicity is debated (Berber, Persian, or Arab in different sources). The single major event of the crossing to Gibraltar and the Battle of Guadalete is well attested in Arabic chronicles, with many of the later details (his speech burning the boats) being later embellishments.

sources: Ibn Abd al-Hakam, Futuh Misr wa al-Maghrib
Encyclopaedia of Islam, 2nd ed., 'Tariq b. Ziyad'

journey.event: Born around 670, probably to a Berber family of the Nafza or Zenata tribes in North Africa, though Arab and Persian origins are also reported in different sources. The exact place is unknown.
journey.source: Ibn Abd al-Hakam; Encyclopaedia of Islam

journey.event: Enters the service of the Umayyad governor of North Africa, Musa ibn Nusayr. Converts to Islam (or is born Muslim, depending on source). Rises in the Muslim military and is appointed governor of Tangier on the Atlantic coast of Morocco around 710.

Made governor of Tangier and the western Maghrib by Musa ibn Nusayr, with a mixed Arab-Berber garrison. Tangier is the closest major Muslim base to the Iberian peninsula, separated only by the narrow strait that will later bear his name.

Receives appeals from Julian, the Byzantine or Visigothic count of Ceuta, who has a personal grievance against the Visigothic king Roderic of Toledo. With Musa's permission, in summer 710 Tariq sends a small reconnaissance force of about 400 men under Tarif ibn Malik across the strait to test the defences. The expedition lands near what is now Tarifa (named after Tarif) and returns with booty.
journey.source: Ibn Abd al-Hakam; Al-Maqqari, Nafh al-Tib

journey.event: In April or May 711, leads the main invasion force of about 7,000 men (mostly Berbers) across the strait in ships supplied by Julian. Lands at the great rock that will later be called Jabal Tariq (Tariq's mountain), the modern Gibraltar. Establishes a base of operations.

On 19 July 711 (or thereabouts) confronts the army of King Roderic of the Visigoths at the Battle of Guadalete (also called Wadi Lakka or Wadi Bakka), in southern Spain. Although heavily outnumbered, Tariq's forces win a decisive victory. Roderic is killed or disappears in the rout. Visigothic Spain effectively collapses.
journey.source: Ibn Abd al-Hakam; Al-Maqqari, Nafh al-Tib

journey.event: After Guadalete, divides his forces and pushes rapidly north. Detachments take Cordoba, Malaga, Granada, and Ecija in the months following the battle. Resistance is local and uncoordinated.

Marches on the Visigothic capital Toledo in central Spain and takes it without serious resistance. According to legend, finds the famous 'Table of Solomon' and other treasures of the kings in the city. Establishes Toledo as his base for the conquest of the rest of the peninsula.
journey.source: Ibn Abd al-Hakam; Al-Maqqari, Nafh al-Tib

journey.event: In June 712 his superior Musa ibn Nusayr crosses the strait with a larger Arab army of about 18,000 men, jealous of Tariq's success. Musa takes a different route, capturing Seville and Merida. The two commanders meet at Toledo in 713. Sources later report that Musa publicly humiliated and even flogged Tariq for taking too much of the credit and treasure.

Together with Musa, pushes north into the territory of the modern Basque country, Asturias, and across the Pyrenees. By 714 most of the peninsula except the mountainous north is under Muslim control. The whole conquest has taken less than three years.

In 714 the Umayyad caliph al-Walid I, alarmed by reports of insubordination and rivalry, summons both Musa and Tariq back to Damascus. They travel overland across the Maghrib and Egypt, bringing the Visigothic spoils with them, and arrive at the caliph's court in Damascus in 715.

In Damascus, al-Walid dies and is succeeded by his brother Sulayman, who is hostile to Musa. Musa is disgraced. Tariq's fate is less clear: most sources say he too was sidelined and ended his days in obscurity in Damascus, possibly in poverty.

Dies in obscurity in Damascus around 720. The exact date and circumstances are not preserved. Despite his historical insignificance after the conquest, his name lives on in Jabal Tariq (Gibraltar), one of the most strategically important geographical features in the world.
